question: The CKM (Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa) matrix appears in charged-current weak interactions. What is it and why does it exist?

options: Unitary $3 \times 3$ matrix $V_{ij}$ rotating quark flavour eigenstates to mass eigenstates: charged-current vertex is $(g/\sqrt 2) \bar u_i \gamma^\mu V_{ij} d_j W_\mu^+$; with 3 generations has 3 mixing angles + 1 CP-violating phase; arises because Yukawa matrices $Y_u, Y_d$ not simultaneously diagonalisable
$2 \times 2$ matrix of Cabibbo angle only; no Kobayashi-Maskawa phase (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship)
Real orthogonal matrix; no CP violation possible (an outdated view superseded by later work) (not supported by the evidence) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)
Diagonal matrix; no flavour mixing in SM (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this conflates cause and effect) (true only as a special case, not in general)
passive.explanation: In SM, quark mass eigenstates $\neq$ weak (flavour) eigenstates because Yukawas $Y_u, Y_d$ are general complex matrices. Diagonalise: $Y_u = U_L^u Y_u^{\rm diag} U_R^{u\dagger}$, $Y_d = U_L^d Y_d^{\rm diag} U_R^{d\dagger}$. Charged current $W^+(\bar u_L\gamma^\mu d_L)$ in mass basis: $W^+\bar u^{\rm mass}_L\gamma^\mu (U_L^{u\dagger} U_L^d)d^{\rm mass}_L = W^+ \bar u V_{\rm CKM} d$. With $N$ generations: $V$ is $N\times N$ unitary $\Rightarrow N^2$ parameters; rephasing absorbs $2N - 1$; leaves $N(N-1)/2$ angles + $(N-1)(N-2)/2$ phases. For $N = 3$: 3 angles + 1 phase. For $N = 2$ (Cabibbo): 1 angle, no phase, no CP violation.

active.prompt: Match each CKM matrix element with its dominant measurement:

pairs.definition: $K \to \pi\ell\nu$ semileptonic ($K_{\ell 3}$); also $\tau \to K\nu_\tau$; tests CKM unitarity
Inclusive $B \to X_c\ell\nu$ and exclusive $B \to D^*\ell\nu$ (HQET); $\sim 2\%$ precision
Inclusive $B \to X_u\ell\nu$ (charmless); long-standing inclusive vs exclusive tension

active.explanation: Magnitudes obey strong hierarchy: $V_{ud}, V_{cs}, V_{tb} \approx 1$; off-diagonal Cabibbo-like $V_{us}, V_{cd} \approx 0.22$; second off-diagonal $V_{cb}, V_{ts} \approx 0.04$; smallest $V_{ub}, V_{td} \approx 0.004$. Wolfenstein parametrisation: $V_{us} = \lambda \approx 0.225$, $V_{cb} = A\lambda^2$, $V_{ub} = A\lambda^3(\rho - i\eta)$ — captures hierarchy.

question: Why did Kobayashi-Maskawa propose a third generation in 1973, predicting bottom and top quarks?

options: With only 2 generations ($u, d, s$ + GIM's $c$): CKM is $2\times 2$ real orthogonal — no CP-violating phase possible. To accommodate observed CP violation in neutral kaons ($\varepsilon_K$, 1964), need at least 3 generations giving complex $3\times 3$ CKM with one physical phase. Predicted $b$ (discovered 1977 at Fermilab) and $t$ (discovered 1995 at CDF/D0)

Three generations were postulated to explain quark confinement (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (a common misconception) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification)

Predicted from grand unification, not CP violation (this describes a different phenomenon) (this conflates cause and effect) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (not supported by the evidence)

Two generations would give too much CP violation, three needed to suppress it (this reverses the actual relationship) (true only as a special case, not in general) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Cronin-Fitch (1964): $K_L \to \pi\pi$ — first evidence of CP violation in nature, characterised by parameter $\varepsilon_K \approx 2.2 \times 10^{-3}$. Kobayashi-Maskawa (1973): proved a $2\times 2$ Cabibbo mixing matrix cannot accommodate CP violation (real entries). With $N$ generations, get $(N-1)(N-2)/2$ CP-violating phases: $N = 2 \Rightarrow 0$; $N = 3 \Rightarrow 1$. Therefore postulated $b$ and $t$ to complete 3rd generation. Bottom discovered 1977 (Upsilon at Fermilab, Lederman et al.); top discovered 1995 (CDF, D0 — $m_t \approx 173$ GeV). KM awarded Nobel 2008 for this prediction. Now: CP violation observed in $K, B, B_s, D$ systems — all consistent with single CKM phase.

question: The unitarity triangle is a graphical way to test CKM unitarity. What does it test?

options: From unitarity $\sum_k V_{ik}V_{jk}^* = 0$ for $i\neq j$; the $bd$ triangle $V_{ud}V_{ub}^* + V_{cd}V_{cb}^* + V_{td}V_{tb}^* = 0$ has three terms of comparable size, forming a non-degenerate triangle in complex plane; angles $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ measured in $B$ decays; all sides + angles must be consistent — over-constrained test for new physics
Triangle inequality on quark masses (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception) (true only as a special case, not in general) (this reverses the actual relationship)

Triangle of generations: $u, d, s$ corners (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (not supported by the evidence) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Test of triangle anomaly cancellation (this conflates cause and effect) (this describes a different phenomenon) (an outdated view superseded by later work) (a common misconception)
passive.explanation: There are 6 such unitarity triangles (one per off-diagonal pair); the $bd$ triangle is most often plotted because all three sides are $O(\lambda^3)$ — comparable, well-shaped triangle. Angles: $\beta = \arg(-V_{cd}V_{cb}^*/V_{td}V_{tb}^*)$, $\alpha = \arg(-V_{td}V_{tb}^*/V_{ud}V_{ub}^*)$, $\gamma = \arg(-V_{ud}V_{ub}^*/V_{cd}V_{cb}^*)$. Measurements: $\sin 2\beta$ from $B_d \to J/\psi K_S$ ($\sin 2\beta = 0.70$); $\alpha$ from $B \to \pi\pi, \rho\rho$; $\gamma$ from $B \to DK$. Sides: $|V_{ub}/V_{cb}|$ ratio and $\Delta m_d/\Delta m_s$ from oscillation. Closure test: angles + sides over-constrained $\Rightarrow$ probes new physics in loops contributing to $\Delta F = 2$ amplitudes.

question: The Jarlskog invariant $J$ characterises CP violation in the CKM matrix. What is it and what's its magnitude?

options: $J = \Im(V_{us}V_{cb}V_{ub}^*V_{cs}^*)$ (any rephasing-invariant quartic product equals $\pm J$); measures area of unitarity triangle; $J \approx 3 \times 10^{-5}$ in SM; CP violation in any process is proportional to $J$ times appropriate kinematic factors; far too small for matter-antimatter asymmetry of universe
$J = \det V_{\rm CKM}$ (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (a common misconception) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
$J = $ Cabibbo angle in radians (this reverses the actual relationship) (not supported by the evidence) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (this describes a different phenomenon)

$J$ is undefined; CP violation cannot be quantified by single invariant (true only as a special case, not in general) (this conflates cause and effect) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
passive.explanation: Jarlskog (1985): rephasing-invariant measure of CP violation, $J = \Im(V_{ij}V_{kl}V_{il}^*V_{kj}^*)$ for any choice $(i\neq k, j\neq l)$ — value same up to sign. SM value: $J \approx (3.0 \pm 0.1)\times 10^{-5}$. Maximum possible if all mixings maximal: $J_{\rm max} = 1/(6\sqrt 3) \approx 0.1$ — actual $J$ tiny because of CKM hierarchy. CP-violating amplitude differences $\propto J\cdot(\text{mass}^2 \text{ differences})$. Baryon asymmetry of universe (BAU) $\eta_B \sim 10^{-10}$: CKM contribution $\sim J\times\Delta_q/T^{12} \sim 10^{-20}$ — many orders too small. Therefore: new sources of CP violation needed for baryogenesis (leptogenesis, BSM physics).

question: Neutral meson mixing ($K\bar K, B\bar B, B_s\bar B_s, D\bar D$) probes CKM via box diagrams. What's the SM prediction?

options: Mass splitting $\Delta m_q \propto G_F^2 M_W^2 f_{B_q}^2 B_{B_q}|V_{tq}^*V_{tb}|^2 S_0(x_t)$ with Inami-Lim function $S_0(x_t)$, $x_t = m_t^2/M_W^2$; box diagram dominated by top quark in loop. $\Delta m_d \approx 0.51$/ps, $\Delta m_s \approx 17.7$/ps; ratio gives $|V_{td}/V_{ts}|^2$ with reduced hadronic uncertainty
Mixing is forbidden in the SM (this describes a different phenomenon) (not supported by the evidence) (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (true only as a special case, not in general)
Mixing comes from tree-level $W$ exchange (this reverses the actual relationship) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
Box diagrams give $\Delta m \propto m_d^4$ (inconsistent with standard results in the field) (this conflates cause and effect) (a common misconception) (this describes a different phenomenon)
passive.explanation: $B_q\bar B_q$ mixing via 4 box diagrams (two W exchanges, $t$ usually dominant due to large $m_t$). Effective Hamiltonian: $\mathcal H_{\rm eff} = (G_F^2/16\pi^2)M_W^2 (V_{tq}^*V_{tb})^2 S_0(x_t) C(\mu) \mathcal O$ with $\mathcal O = (\bar q b)_{V-A}(\bar qb)_{V-A}$. Hadronic matrix element $\langle\bar B|\mathcal O|B\rangle = (8/3)f_{B_q}^2 B_{B_q} M_{B_q}^2$ — needs lattice. Measured (LHCb, Tevatron, B factories): $\Delta m_d = 0.5065$/ps, $\Delta m_s = 17.765$/ps. Ratio $\Delta m_d/\Delta m_s = (|V_{td}|^2/|V_{ts}|^2)\xi^2 M_{B_d}/M_{B_s}$ where SU(3)-breaking $\xi = 1.21$ from lattice — gives $|V_{td}/V_{ts}| \approx 0.21$. Charm $D\bar D$ mixing: very small (GIM-suppressed), $x_D \sim 0.4\%$ observed.

question: The Wolfenstein parametrisation expresses the CKM hierarchy as expansion in $\lambda \approx 0.225$. Write it out.

options: $V_{\rm CKM} = \begin{pmatrix}1-\lambda^2/2 & \lambda & A\lambda^3(\rho - i\eta)\\ -\lambda & 1-\lambda^2/2 & A\lambda^2 \\ A\lambda^3(1-\rho - i\eta) & -A\lambda^2 & 1\end{pmatrix} + O(\lambda^4)$ with $A \approx 0.81$, $\rho \approx 0.16$, $\eta \approx 0.36$; CP violation in $\eta$
$V_{ij} = \lambda^{|i-j|}$ for all entries (true only as a special case, not in general) (not supported by the evidence) (inconsistent with standard results in the field)
Diagonal matrix with $\lambda$ on diagonal (a frequently-taught but incorrect simplification) (this describes a different phenomenon) (this reverses the actual relationship)
$V_{\rm CKM}$ has no useful expansion parameter (a common misconception) (this confuses two distinct concepts) (an outdated view superseded by later work)
passive.explanation: Wolfenstein parametrisation (1983): exposes the hierarchy $|V_{us}| \sim \lambda$, $|V_{cb}| \sim \lambda^2$, $|V_{ub}|, |V_{td}| \sim \lambda^3$. Convenient for analysing CP asymmetries to needed order. Four real parameters: $\lambda = |V_{us}|$, $A = |V_{cb}|/\lambda^2$, plus complex $\bar\rho + i\bar\eta = -V_{ud}V_{ub}^*/(V_{cd}V_{cb}^*)$ (apex of $bd$ unitarity triangle). Current fits (CKMfitter, UTfit): $\lambda = 0.2251(7)$, $A = 0.826(12)$, $\bar\rho = 0.159(10)$, $\bar\eta = 0.348(10)$. All measurements consistent — global SM CKM picture works at $\sim 10\%$ precision across many independent processes. New physics constraints: contributions to $\Delta F = 2$ amplitudes bounded to $\lesssim 10\%$ of SM, pushing BSM scales above $\sim 10^4$ TeV (generically).
